metadata.title: Six Agent Variants: How to Measure What Prompt Changes Actually Do

beats.narration_text: You tweak the system prompt. Does it actually help — or did you just shuffle tokens? This is Liam, in for Bear.

props.segment: Six Agent Variants: How to Measure What Prompt Changes Actually Do
props.command: I've iterated my agent's system prompt six times. How do I know which version actually improved the output — without manually reviewing every run?

beats.narration_text: The question: how do you know a prompt change improves output without manually inspecting every variant — and spending hours doing it?

The answer is a two-layer eval. Layer one: structural. Parse the output — in this case a PowerPoint file's XML — and measure layout metrics programmatically. Font consistency, alignment, color contrast. Objective numbers. Layer two: semantic. Render the output and use an LLM as a judge, grading visual impact and usability. Now every prompt iteration runs against the same rubric. Not vibed — measured.

Six variants, same 10-task eval suite. Naive agent: Comic Sans, poor contrast, cluttered layout. Add a typography hint — two sentences — and re-run. The structural grader shows readability up, text overflow down. Add a palette constraint: contrast scores improve. Add a density pass: information-per-slide tightens. Add a QA loop: the agent catches its own errors before the grader does. Each delta is visible. Each improvement is traceable to the change that caused it. The score bars are the proof.

The accumulation is the point. Each variant adds one change. Each change gets one measured delta. By variant six, you know exactly which two-sentence prompt addition gave you the biggest readability gain — and which palette change introduced a regression you can now fix.

Here is how the scoring actually works. Layer one is fast and free: parse the output XML, check the JSON schema, run the structural rules — font names, alignment margins, contrast ratios. Each check returns pass or fail. No model call. You get a structural score in milliseconds. Layer two is slower and richer: render the output, hand it to an LLM judge with a grader prompt, receive a zero-to-ten score per criterion. Visual impact, information density, layout clarity. The semantic grader catches what the structural grader cannot: whether the slide actually communicates. Both scores flow down into a single row in the results table. Structure first — it eliminates the obvious failures before you pay for a model judgment.

Here is the cumulative improvement, laid out as a waterfall. The naive agent starts at 42 percent. Add a ReAct reasoning loop — plus 14 points, you are at 56. Attach a memory store — plus 8, now 64. Add a critic pass — plus 9, now 73. Introduce tool planning — plus 5, now 78. Add output formatting constraints — plus 3 — and the final score is 81 percent. Each bar is one change. Each delta is measured, not estimated. The total gain — 39 points from baseline — came from six targeted interventions. No single change dominated. The compound of small, isolated, measured improvements is what moved the needle from mediocre to strong.

props.sparkLine: Every structured change is a measurable gain.

beats.narration_text: Three moments demand an eval run. Before you deploy: establish a baseline. Without a number, you have nothing to beat. You cannot claim improvement if you never measured the starting point. After any prompt change: this is the most common trigger. Every edit to the system prompt is a hypothesis — the eval tests it. If you changed the prompt and skipped the eval, you are flying blind. On regression reports: when production quality drops, run the eval immediately. It tells you which metric regressed, which variant introduced the problem, and where to look. The middle card is highlighted because it is the one practitioners skip. They change the prompt, it feels better, they ship. The eval is what separates intuition from signal.

Let's recap with Claude. Split the eval into two layers: structural parses the output's metrics programmatically; semantic uses an LLM judge on the rendered result. Six variants, same 10-task suite, one change per variant. Every prompt iteration is now measured against the same rubric. Not vibed — scored.

artifactLines: Structural grader: parse the output XML, measure layout metrics programmatically.
Semantic grader: render the output, use an LLM judge to score visual impact.
Six variants, same 10-task suite — one change per variant isolates the delta.
Every prompt iteration runs against the same rubric. Not vibed — measured.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt: 'Run my agent on the same 10-task eval before and after this prompt change, and grade both structurally and semantically — did it improve?' What you are pushing on is the difference between a gut feeling about a prompt change and a measurable verdict. Watch what the structural grader catches that you wouldn't have caught reading the output: font consistency across slides, alignment drift, contrast ratios. Then watch what the LLM judge catches that the structural grader misses: whether the layout actually communicates the idea, or just technically passes the metrics. Both layers together give you a signal worth trusting. Take this prompt, run it on your own agent, and see what the eval reveals. Liam, in for Bear.
